title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 3.1 What does a code chunk look like?
text:
3.1 What does a code chunk look like?
An empty code chunk looks like this:

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
3.2 Enter and run code

Type R code inside code chunks
Select code you want to run, by

placing the cursor in the line of code you want to run,
or highlighting the code you want to run

Run selected code by

clicking on the Run button in the top right corner of the scripting window and choosing Run Selected Line(s),
or typing one of the following key combinations:





Mac
ctrl + return


PC
command + return




Where does the output appear?
Run all code in a chunk by

by clicking the play button in the top right corner of the chunk

The code output appears below the code chunk


The output should also appear in the Console.
Settings can be changed so that the output appears only in the Console and not below the code chunk:

Select {{< fa solid gear >}} (to right of Render button) and then Chunk Output in Console.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
3.3 Create a code chunk
3 options to create a code chunk

Click on “+C” at top right of the editor window, or
Keyboard shortcut




Mac
Command + Option + I


PC
Ctrl + Alt + I




Visual editor: Select Insert -> Executable Cell -> R

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
4.1 Objects and <-

An object is a named box that stores something: a number, a dataset, a result – anything.
We create objects with the assignment arrow <- (keyboard shortcut: option/alt + -):


my_number <- 10   # store 10 in an object called my_number
my_number         # type the name to see what's inside

[1] 10



The object now shows up in your Environment tab (top right).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
4.2 Functions and arguments

A function does something. You run it by writing its name followed by parentheses:

function_name(argument1, argument2, ...)

The values inside the parentheses are the arguments: they tell the function what to work on and how.


sqrt(16)            # sqrt() takes one argument

[1] 4

round(3.14159, 1)   # round() takes two: the number, and the digits to keep

[1] 3.1



Arguments often have names, e.g. round(3.14159, digits = 1).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
4.3 c(): combine values

c() (“combine”) glues several values into a single vector (a list of values of the same type).
You’ll use it constantly – for example, to list the levels of a factor later today:


c(1, 2, 3)

[1] 1 2 3

c("9th", "10th", "11th", "12th")

[1] "9th"  "10th" "11th" "12th"

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 4.4 $: grab one column from a dataset
text:
4.4 $: grab one column from a dataset

Use dataset$column_name to pull a single column out of a dataset:


# (once we've loaded a dataset, e.g. toy_data)
toy_data$age        # the age column of toy_data
class(toy_data$age) # what type is that column?


We’ll mostly work on whole datasets with the tidyverse, but $ is handy for a quick peek at one column.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
5.1 Getting help

Put a ? before a function name to open its help page (in the Help tab):


?mean        # help page for the mean() function
?read_excel


Other great resources:

the cheatsheets (Help menu > Cheat Sheets, or posit.co/resources/cheatsheets)
R for Data Science (2e), a free beginner-friendly book
a search engine: paste the error message in!

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
5.2 Reading an error message

A red message in the console is normal: it’s R telling you what went wrong, not a sign you failed.
Two of the most common beginner errors:








Message
Usually means




object 'x' not found
A name R doesn’t know: a typo, or you haven’t created/loaded it yet


could not find function "..."
The function’s package isn’t loaded: run library(thepackage)




Check first: spelling, capitalization, matching () and "", and whether you ran the earlier code (objects and library() calls) in this session.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
6.1 What are R Packages?
A good analogy for R packages is that they are like apps you can download onto a smart phone

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
6.2 Installing packages

Packages contain additional functions and data

Two options to install packages:

install.packages() or
The “Packages” tab in Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer window


install.packages("janitor")   # only do this ONCE, use quotes


Only install packages once (unless you want to update them)
Installed from Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN) = package mothership

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 6.3 Install packages used in this workshop
text:
6.3 Install packages used in this workshop

Run the code below to install packages:


install.packages("tidyverse")  # this takes a while to install
install.packages("writexl")
install.packages("rstatix") 
install.packages("janitor")
install.packages("naniar")
install.packages("gt")
install.packages("gtsummary")


It is VERY IMPORTANT that you install these packages in order to run the code for the workshop!

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
6.4 Load packages with library() function

Use the library() function to load each required package.
Packages need to be loaded every time you open Rstudio.

The code to load packages MUST be in your qmd file.
Tip: at the top of your qmd file, create a chunk that loads all of the R packages you want to use in that file.



library(janitor)    # run this every time you open Rstudio


Check to see whether a package has been loaded:

Go to Packages tab
If box to left of package name is checked off, then it’s loaded

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 6.5 Load packages used in this workshop
text:
6.5 Load packages used in this workshop

Run the code below in your R session


library(tidyverse)
library(writexl)
library(rstatix)
library(janitor)
library(naniar)
library(gt)
library(gtsummary)
library(readxl)  # part of tidyverse, but not the tidyverse core


Note that the package names do not need quotes around them when using the library() function, but they are required when using the install.packages() function.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
7.1 Dataset example (toy data)

Use Excel to open the dataset toy_data.xlsx that is located in the data folder.
Terminology

Rows are usually observations (i.e. a person’s measurements)
Columns are usually variables or measures

How many observations are in this dataset?
What are the variable types in this dataset?

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 7.2 Open the .Rproj so file paths work
text:
7.2 Open the .Rproj so file paths work

Our code loads data with a relative path: read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx").

This means “find the data folder inside my current working folder.”

When you open the workshop by double-clicking the .Rproj file, RStudio sets that working folder for you, so "data/..." points to the right place.
If you see an error like cannot open file 'data/toy_data.xlsx': No such file or directory, it almost always means RStudio isn’t pointed at the project folder.

Fix: open the .Rproj file (at the top of the unzipped workshop folder), then re-run your code.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
7.3 Import data into R

We can import data from many file types, including .csv, .txt., .xlsx, plus SAS or Stata files
Once imported, R typically stores data as data frames, or tibbles if using the {tidyverse} package.

These are examples of what we call object types in R.
For our purposes, these are essentially the same, and we will often use the terms interchangeably.
These workshop slides will usually use the term dataset to generally refer to either a data frame or tibble.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 7.4 Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: point & click option
text:
7.4 Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: point & click option
SAVE THE CODE after using the point & click option

In RStudio’s Files window, open the data folder and

then click on the dataset toy_data.xlsx.

Select the Import Dataset... option,

Import Options: Type in NA in the “NA:” box, and
then click on the Import button on the bottom right of the pop-up window.

Copy the code from the console used to load the file into your script (omitting the + sign):


toy_data <- read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx", na = "NA")

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 7.5 Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: code option
text:
7.5 Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: code option

The point and click option used the read_excel function from the readxl package to load the xlsx file.
If comfortable with coding and the data, we can skip the point and click and directly write the code ourselves.
When loading the dataset, we need to specify where the xlsx file is located, which in this case is in the folder called data.
No matter which option we use, the code to load the data MUST be in your qmd.


# library(readxl) # commented out since loaded above already
toy_data <- read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx", na = "NA")


This code assigns the output of the read_excel() function (data) into an object named toy_data
The <- is the assignment operator
After loading, the name of the dataset object toy_data will be in your Environment tab
To learn more about loading in Excel files, read the readxl intro vignette
Also check out the readxl cheatsheet (this will download a pdf).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
7.6 View the toy_data dataset

Run the View function below within RStudio.


View(toy_data)


A new tab in the scripting window will appear with the toy_data dataset.
Note that the code chunk in the qmd file specifies #| eval: FALSE so that this code is not run while rendering the qmd file.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
7.7 What are the variable “types”?







R variable type
Description




dbl: double
numbers; also num or int for numbers or integers


chr: character
text, “strings”


fct: factor
categorical variables stored with levels (groups)


lgl: logical
boolean (TRUE, FALSE)




glimpse(toy_data)

Rows: 20
Columns: 11
$ id                     <dbl> 335340, 638618, 922382, 923122, 923963, 925603,…
$ age                    <chr> "17 years old", "16 years old", "14 years old",…
$ sex                    <chr> "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Ma…
$ grade                  <chr> "10th", "9th", "9th", "9th", "10th", "10th", "1…
$ race4                  <chr> "White", NA, "White", "White", "Black or Africa…
$ bmi                    <dbl> 27.5671, 29.3495, 18.1827, 21.3754, 19.5988, 22…
$ weight_kg              <dbl> 66.23, 84.82, 57.61, 60.33, 63.50, 70.31, 45.36…
$ text_while_driving_30d <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, "0 days…
$ smoked_ever            <chr> NA, "Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No…
$ bullied_past_12mo      <lgl> NA, NA, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, …
$ height_m               <dbl> 1.550000, 1.699999, 1.779999, 1.680001, 1.79999…

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
7.8 Getting to know the dataset

7.8.1 How many rows and columns?

# dim is for dimensions
# Output: # of rows, # of columns
dim(toy_data)   

[1] 20 11

nrow(toy_data)

[1] 20

ncol(toy_data)

[1] 11




7.8.2 What are the variable names?

names(toy_data)

 [1] "id"                     "age"                    "sex"                   
 [4] "grade"                  "race4"                  "bmi"                   
 [7] "weight_kg"              "text_while_driving_30d" "smoked_ever"           
[10] "bullied_past_12mo"      "height_m"

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
10.1 View the YRBSS dataset

Run the View function below within RStudio.

A new tab in the scripting window will appear with the yrbss_data dataset.

Note that the code chunk specifies #| eval: FALSE so that this code is not run while rendering the .qmd file.


View(yrbss_data)

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
12.1 get_summary_stats() from the {rstatix} package

The {rstatix} package has a useful function called get_summary_stats() for quick statistics of quantitative data


# library(rstatix)

yrbss_data |> 
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    ) 

# A tibble: 2 × 10
  variable     n   min   max median   iqr  mean    sd    se    ci
  <fct>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 bmi      13542  13.2  53.9   22.3  5.52  23.5  4.99 0.043 0.084
2 stweight 13443  27.7 181.    64.0 19.0   67.5 16.9  0.145 0.285



The type options are

type = c("full", "common", "robust", "five_number", "mean_sd", "mean_se", "mean_ci", "median_iqr", "median_mad", "quantile", "mean", "median", "min", "max")

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
12.2 Stratified summaries (and more on |>)

Now let’s summarize the BMI and weight variables by a strata subgroup.
We do this with the group_by() function
Notice that we now have two |>:

data |> group_by(...) |> get_summary_stats(...)
With the pipe symbol |> we can string together many functions in the order we want them applied. Example:

first tell R what dataset to use,
then have R group (stratify) the data by sex,
and then calculate summary statistics




yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex) |>
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    )

Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
ℹ In argument: `sd = stats::sd(.value., na.rm = TRUE)`.
Caused by warning:
! There were 2 warnings in `dplyr::summarise()`.
The first warning was:
ℹ In argument: `min = min(.value., na.rm = TRUE)`.
Caused by warning in `min()`:
! no non-missing arguments to min; returning Inf
ℹ Run `dplyr::last_dplyr_warnings()` to see the 1 remaining warning.


# A tibble: 4 × 11
  sex    variable     n   min   max median   iqr  mean    sd    se    ci
  <chr>  <fct>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Female bmi       6622  13.2  53.3   22.1  5.33  23.3  4.96 0.061 0.119
2 Female stweight  6542  27.7 181.    59.0 15.9   61.7 14.2  0.175 0.343

4 Male   stweight  6901  35.4 181.    69.4 20.0   73.1 17.4  0.209 0.41

text:
12.3 Stratify by 2 variables

We can specify more than one variable within group_by() to stratify the summaries by more variables


yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex, grade) |>
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    )

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
12.5 summarize() function

Another option for summarizing numeric data is the summarize() function
This is helpful if you want to create customized summaries
See examples from BSTA 526 on how to use summarize()

Below are brief examples to give you an idea of how summarize() works:

Simple summarize() example

one variable, one summary statistic, one grouping variable



yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(grade) |>
  summarize(mean = mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  gt()







grade
mean




10th
23.22864


11th
23.79501


12th
24.17164


9th
22.81397


NA
23.52221








Advanced summarize() example

two variables, two summary statistics, two grouping variables



yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex, grade) |>
  summarize(across(.cols = c(bmi, stweight), 
                   .fns = list(
                     mean = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
                     sd = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
                   ))) |> 
  gt()

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by sex and grade.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
13.1 Background

The newer base R pipe |> was introduced in 2021 with the release of R 4.1.0.
The original magrittr pipe %>%

comes from the {magrittr} package and
became popular through the {tidyverse}.

Using the base R pipe |> does not require loading the {tidyverse} package (which includes {magrittr}),

while using the magrittr pipe %>% does require {tidyverse}.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
13.2 Similarities

They work the same when the left-hand side becomes the first argument of the next function.
Example:

Using |>

yrbss_data |> dim()

[1] 20000     8


Using %>%

yrbss_data %>% dim()

[1] 20000     8

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
13.3 Differences
When do |> and %>% behave differently?

13.3.1 Simple example

|> is stricter and expects an actual function call on the right


yrbss_data |> dim()

[1] 20000     8



yrbss_data |> dim

Error in dim: The pipe operator requires a function call as RHS (<input>:1:15)



yrbss_data |> dim() works, but yrbss_data |> dim does not:

With %>%, we can omit the () after the function name:

yrbss_data %>% dim()

[1] 20000     8

yrbss_data %>% dim

[1] 20000     8




13.3.2 Advanced example

Code below runs a linear regression model with dependent variable bmi and independent variable age.
Using %>%:

note that for the data argument a . is used as the placeholder for the dataset yrbss_data since it’s already being “fed into” the model.



yrbss_data %>% lm(bmi ~ age, data = .)


Call:
lm(formula = bmi ~ age, data = .)

Coefficients:
             (Intercept)           age13 years old           age14 years old  
                 22.3834                   -1.3292                   -0.1210  
         age15 years old           age16 years old           age17 years old  
                  0.5009                    1.0989                    1.5347  
age18 years old or older  
                  2.1176  



Using |>:
Using a . as as a placeholder for the data does not work with the newer base R |>:


yrbss_data |>  lm(bmi ~ age, data = .)

Error:
! object '.' not found



Starting with R 4.3.0, we can use a placeholder but it’s an underscore_:


yrbss_data |>  lm(bmi ~ age, data = _)


Call:
lm(formula = bmi ~ age, data = yrbss_data)

Coefficients:
             (Intercept)           age13 years old           age14 years old  
                 22.3834                   -1.3292                   -0.1210  
         age15 years old           age16 years old           age17 years old  
                  0.5009                    1.0989                    1.5347  
age18 years old or older  
                  2.1176  




13.3.3 More advanced examples
See Learn more section below for resources with more examples.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
13.4 For this workshop
We will use |> because

it is built into R,
requires no extra package, and
works well for most {tidyverse} code you will write as beginners

Our older workshops and many reference materials use %>%.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
13.5 Learn more

Differences between the base R and magrittr pipes - Tidyverse blog post by Hadley Wickham
Discussion of differences on Stack Overflow
R 4.1.0 pipe overview
Comparing pipes: Base-R |> vs {magrittr} %>%

this resource includes examples and
also how to change the “default” pipe in RStudio from %>% to |> (keyboard shortcut will use |>)

14.2 Contingency tables (two-way tables)

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.1 Histograms

ggplot(data = yrbss_data,    # specify the dataset
       # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used
       aes(x = bmi)) +  
  geom_histogram() # type of plot

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.


Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_bin()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.2 Boxplots

Recall that the whiskers’ ends are the minimum and maximum values
The box is from the first to third quartiles, with the line inside the box at the median

BMI:
horizontal boxplot (specify x = ...)

ggplot(data = yrbss_data,   # dataset
  # aes: specify vars being used
       aes(x = bmi)) +   
  geom_boxplot()   # type of plot

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).









Weight: vertical boxplot (specify y = ...)

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
# use y instead of x in aes for vertical:
  aes(y = stweight)) + 
  geom_boxplot() 

Warning: Removed 6557 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.3 Stratified boxplots (plus axis labels & title!)

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(
         # horizontal boxplots for BMI (x=):
         x = bmi,
         # stratified vertically (y=) by age:
         y = age
         )) + 
  geom_boxplot() + 
  # Add on labels and a title:
  labs(x = "BMI", 
       y = "Age", 
       title =  "BMI distributions by age category"
       )  

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.4 Add color using fill

Also remove the NA category with drop_na() (we will talk about this in later slides)
What’s the difference in use of fill= here?


ggplot(
  data = yrbss_data |> drop_na(age), #NEW!
  aes(
    x = bmi,
    y = age,
    fill = age  # NEW!
  )) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  # remove legend:
  theme(legend.position="none") 

Warning: Removed 6253 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).










ggplot(
  data = yrbss_data |> drop_na(age), #NEW!
  aes(
    x = bmi,
    y = age,
    fill = sex  # NEW!
  )) + 
  geom_boxplot() 

Warning: Removed 6253 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.5 Scatterplots

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # specify the dataset
       # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used
       aes(x = stweight,       
           y = bmi
           )) +  
  geom_point()    # type of plot     

Warning: Removed 6559 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.6 Scatterplot with color-coded dots

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used
       aes(x = stweight,      
           y = bmi,  
           color = sex,
           )) +   
  geom_point(alpha = .2) # alpha controls transparency of the points

Warning: Removed 6559 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.7 Color vs. fill vs. alpha

color (or colour) is used for lines and outlines
fill is used for interiors
Note: geom_point() shape default is a point with only an outline and no interior -> we used color and not fill
alpha specifies the opacity / transparency

Values range from 0 to 1
lower alphas are more transparent

See ggplot webpage Colour related aesthetics: colour, fill, and alpha for more information

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
17.8 Your turn: plots

Try itSolution


Using yrbss_data:

Make a boxplot of bmi stratified by grade.
Make a scatterplot of bmi vs stweight, color-coded by sex.




# boxplot of bmi by grade
ggplot(yrbss_data, aes(x = bmi, y = grade)) +
  geom_boxplot()

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).








# scatterplot of bmi vs stweight, colored by sex
ggplot(yrbss_data, aes(x = stweight, y = bmi, color = sex)) +
  geom_point(alpha = .2)

Warning: Removed 6559 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
18.1 Barplots
By default, barplots display counts (frequencies) on the vertical axis:

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age)) +
  geom_bar() + # specify plot type 
  
  # angle the x-axis labels so that they are not overlapping
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -45, hjust = 0))

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
18.2 Barplots with percents
To show percents, the code is more complicated.

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age)) +
  # specify aesthetics within the barplot to show proportions
  geom_bar(aes(y = after_stat(prop), group = 1)) + 
  
  # Next line converts y-axis labels to percentages instead of proportions
  scale_y_continuous(labels =  scales::percent_format()) +
  # "dodge" the x-axis labels
  scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(n.dodge = 2)) +
  labs(y = "Percent") # default label is prop

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 18.3 Barplots with 2 variables: segmented bar plots
text:
18.3 Barplots with 2 variables: segmented bar plots
Stacked bars showing counts:

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(y = age,
           # specify stacking variable: 
           fill = grade)) +
  geom_bar()








For proportions within each bar:
(added position = "fill" within geom_bar())

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(y = age,
           # specify stacking variable:
           fill = grade)) + 
  geom_bar(position = "fill") 









Note that by using y = age instead of x = age,

the bars of the figure are now horizontal instead of vertical.

With the horizontal bars, we can see the long labels for each category.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
18.4 Side-by-side barplots (dodged)

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age,
           # specify variable for colors 
           fill = grade)) + 
  
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +  # make dodged bars
  
  # angle the x-axis labels so that they are not overlapping
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -15, hjust = 0))

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 18.5 Saving a ggplot as an image file
text:
18.5 Saving a ggplot as an image file
First, create a plot and save it as an R object:

myplot <- ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age,
           # specify variable for colors 
           fill = grade)) + 
  geom_bar() +
  # angle the x-axis labels 
  #so that they are not overlapping
  theme(
    axis.text.x = 
      element_text(angle = -15, hjust = 0))

myplot








Save the plot as a pdf (or “jpeg”, “tiff”, “png”, etc.)

ggsave(
  filename = "output/myplot.pdf",
  plot = myplot,
  device = "pdf",
  width = 5,
  height = 3,
  units = "in",
  dpi = 200)

Can specify the dpi when saving, many journals have dpi requirements.
See [ggsave webpage]{https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/ggsave.html} for more details.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
18.6 * More ggplot topics

Change the theme of the plot

This includes the color scheme, text font and size, and other options.
There are many existing themes to choose from.

Arrange multiple plots as one

patchwork package
Claus O. Wilke’s cowplot package
Shared legend for all plots!

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
18.7 * More ggplot workshop slides

These slides are heavily adapted from R workshops

presented together with Jessica Minnier, PhD
sponsored by OCTRI Biostatistics, Epidemiology, Research & Design (BERD):

Data Visualization with R and ggplot2 2020/05/20

19.2 Visualizing missing data across dataset

This plot helps visualize whether

there are variables with a lot of missing values and/or
participants with a lot of missing values

Each row is an observation
Columns are for the different variables in the dataset
Black = missing, grey = not missing

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
19.3 Missingness heatmap by group

This plot is useful in comparing missingness rates stratified by a variable
Specify the variable to stratify the missingness heatmap by in the fct = option

Example: Percent missingness stratified by age

gg_miss_fct(yrbss_data, fct = age)

UpSet plots are great for finding sets of variables that have a lot of missingess.


gg_miss_upset(yrbss_data, nsets = 10)

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
21.1 Sorting datasets using arrange()
arrange() is a function that lets us sort a dataset by a specified variable.

By default, it sorts in ascending order:


yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(record)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
   record age                     sex   grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
    <dbl> <chr>                   <chr> <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1  30592 <NA>                    <NA>  <NA>  <NA>          <NA>     NA       NA
 2  30593 <NA>                    <NA>  9th   Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…    NA       NA
 3  30595 16 years old            <NA>  10th  White         White    NA       NA
 4  30599 12 years old or younger Male  <NA>  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…    NA       NA
 5  30601 12 years old or younger Male  11th  White         White    NA       NA
 6  30604 12 years old or younger Male  9th   Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…    NA       NA
 7  30605 12 years old or younger Male  9th   All other ra… Asian    NA       NA
 8  30607 12 years old or younger Male  10th  All other ra… Am I…    NA       NA
 9  30608 12 years old or younger Male  9th   All other ra… <NA>     NA       NA
10  30612 14 years old            Male  9th   All other ra… <NA>     NA       NA
# ℹ 19,990 more rows



To sort by descending order, you need to wrap the variable in the desc() function:


yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(desc(record))

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…  17.7     49.9
 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other ra… Asian  19.8     44.4
10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     61.2
# ℹ 19,990 more rows

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
21.2 Sorting datasets by multiple variables

You can also arrange by multiple variables.

The code below sorts by age first, and then within age, we sort by record.



yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(age, record)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
   record age                     sex    grade race4        race7   bmi stweight
    <dbl> <chr>                   <chr>  <chr> <chr>        <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1  30599 12 years old or younger Male   <NA>  Hispanic/La… Hisp…    NA       NA
 2  30601 12 years old or younger Male   11th  White        White    NA       NA
 3  30604 12 years old or younger Male   9th   Hispanic/La… Hisp…    NA       NA
 4  30605 12 years old or younger Male   9th   All other r… Asian    NA       NA
 5  30607 12 years old or younger Male   10th  All other r… Am I…    NA       NA
 6  30608 12 years old or younger Male   9th   All other r… <NA>     NA       NA
 7  36582 12 years old or younger Female 12th  All other r… <NA>     NA       NA
 8  36584 12 years old or younger Female <NA>  Black or Af… Blac…    NA       NA
 9  36585 12 years old or younger Female 12th  All other r… Am I…    NA       NA
10  36586 12 years old or younger Female 9th   Black or Af… Blac…    NA       NA
# ℹ 19,990 more rows



Note that order of variables in arrange() matters!


yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(record, age)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
   record age                     sex   grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
    <dbl> <chr>                   <chr> <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1  30592 <NA>                    <NA>  <NA>  <NA>          <NA>     NA       NA
 2  30593 <NA>                    <NA>  9th   Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…    NA       NA
 3  30595 16 years old            <NA>  10th  White         White    NA       NA
 4  30599 12 years old or younger Male  <NA>  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…    NA       NA
 5  30601 12 years old or younger Male  11th  White         White    NA       NA
 6  30604 12 years old or younger Male  9th   Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…    NA       NA
 7  30605 12 years old or younger Male  9th   All other ra… Asian    NA       NA
 8  30607 12 years old or younger Male  10th  All other ra… Am I…    NA       NA
 9  30608 12 years old or younger Male  9th   All other ra… <NA>     NA       NA
10  30612 14 years old            Male  9th   All other ra… <NA>     NA       NA
# ℹ 19,990 more rows

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
21.3 arrange() after count()-ing categorical data

Another example of when arrange() is useful
We’ve used tabyl() to see what values exist in a categorical variable.
We can also use count() to count all of the unique values for a categorical variable.
This will also show NA category, which is the special missing variable type:


yrbss_data |>
  count(age)

# A tibble: 8 × 2
  age                         n
  <chr>                   <int>
1 12 years old or younger   137
2 13 years old               96
3 14 years old             2026
4 15 years old             4290
5 16 years old             4924
6 17 years old             4988
7 18 years old or older    3334
8 <NA>                      205



Sometimes it helps to arrange by that category if you have many categories to view.
This can also alert us if there are categorical coding mistakes (such as misspellings) in our data.


yrbss_data |>
  count(age) |>
  arrange(n)

# A tibble: 8 × 2
  age                         n
  <chr>                   <int>
1 13 years old               96
2 12 years old or younger   137
3 <NA>                      205
4 14 years old             2026
5 18 years old or older    3334
6 15 years old             4290
7 16 years old             4924
8 17 years old             4988

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 21.4 Saving changes we make to a dataset
text:
21.4 Saving changes we make to a dataset

Note that we have not changed the order of the yrbss_data dataset after arranging:


head(yrbss_data)

# A tibble: 6 × 8
   record age                   sex    grade race4          race7   bmi stweight
    <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>          <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
1  931897 15 years old          Female 10th  White          White  17.2     54.4
2  333862 17 years old          Female 12th  White          White  20.2     57.2
3   36253 18 years old or older Male   11th  Hispanic/Lati… Hisp…  NA       NA  
4 1095530 15 years old          Male   10th  Black or Afri… Blac…  28.0     85.7
5 1303997 14 years old          Male   9th   All other rac… Mult…  24.5     66.7
6  261619 17 years old          Male   9th   All other rac… <NA>   NA       NA  



We need to save our work by re-assigning the sorted/arranged dataset as yrbss_data with the <- operator:


yrbss_data <- yrbss_data |> arrange(desc(record))

yrbss_data

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…  17.7     49.9
 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other ra… Asian  19.8     44.4
10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     61.2
# ℹ 19,990 more rows

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.1 filter()ing quantitative data
filter() is an extremely powerful function. It lets us subset our data according to specific criteria.

Let’s filter on a numeric variable, bmi: all rows with bmi values greater than 40


yrbss_data |>
  filter(bmi > 40)

# A tibble: 160 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1316985 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  42.4    127. 
 2 1316823 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  43.3    118. 
 3 1316755 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  41.0    109. 
 4 1316476 18 years old or older Female 12th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  49.6    104. 
 5 1316448 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other ra… Mult…  41.9    128. 
 6 1316336 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  40.2    113. 
 7 1316330 18 years old or older Female 12th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  40.2    113. 
 8 1316235 18 years old or older Female 12th  <NA>          <NA>   45.1    120. 
 9 1316186 18 years old or older Female <NA>  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  47.6     89.4
10 1315913 17 years old          Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  53.3    142. 
# ℹ 150 more rows



Filter to only include all rows with bmi values equal to 13.9


yrbss_data |>
  filter(bmi == 13.9)

# A tibble: 0 × 8
# ℹ 8 variables: record <dbl>, age <chr>, sex <chr>, grade <chr>, race4 <chr>,
#   race7 <chr>, bmi <dbl>, stweight <dbl>



There are no rows with bmi values equal to 13.9, but bmi was rounded to 4 decimal places:


yrbss_data |>
  filter(bmi == 13.9229)

# A tibble: 1 × 8
   record age          sex   grade race4                    race7   bmi stweight
    <dbl> <chr>        <chr> <chr> <chr>                    <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
1 1096565 15 years old Male  10th  Black or African Americ… Blac…  13.9     56.2



Note the double equal signs!

== is a test of whether two things are equal,
it is not an “assignment” (i.e. x = 5 assigns the value 5 to the object x).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.2 filter()ing cateogrical data

To filter on a categorical variable, we need to know the values (levels) of the variable:


yrbss_data |> tabyl(grade)

 grade    n percent valid_percent
  10th 4907 0.24535     0.2504338
  11th 4891 0.24455     0.2496172
  12th 4577 0.22885     0.2335919
   9th 5219 0.26095     0.2663570
  <NA>  406 0.02030            NA



Note the double equal signs!

== is a test of whether two things are equal,
it is not an “assignment” (i.e. x = 5 assigns the value 5 to the object x).



yrbss_data |>
  filter(grade == "10th") # We must include " " around the value for categorical variables

# A tibble: 4,907 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317010 18 years old or older Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  28.3     61.2
 2 1315996 17 years old          Female 10th  White         White  16.3     44.4
 3 1315971 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  19.6     56.7
 4 1315934 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  23.5     70.3
 5 1315928 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  21.0     45.4
 6 1315926 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  25.9     66.2
 7 1315884 17 years old          Female 10th  White         White  51.4    132. 
 8 1315816 17 years old          Female 10th  All other ra… Mult…  22.2     59.0
 9 1315792 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  21.7     61.2
10 1315776 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  20.6     50.8
# ℹ 4,897 more rows



If we want everything except 10th grade, we use the “not equal” sign !=


yrbss_data |>
  filter(grade != "10th")

# A tibble: 14,687 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…  17.7     49.9
 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other ra… Asian  19.8     44.4
10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     61.2
# ℹ 14,677 more rows

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.3 Filtering requires a little logic

We can chain multiple criteria using the & (AND) or | (OR) operators.

But we need to review a little logic before we do this.

If we wanted to return participants who identified as

Female and are in 10th grade,
we would use an & to chain these criteria together:


sex == "Female" & grade == "10th"

Note that SPELLING AND CASE MATTER!

You must write the name of the column exactly and
also the value (i.e. “Female” not “female”) exactly.

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.4 Filtering with & (AND) criteria

yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female" & 
           grade == "10th")

# A tibble: 2,332 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317010 18 years old or older Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  28.3     61.2
 2 1315996 17 years old          Female 10th  White         White  16.3     44.4
 3 1315971 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  19.6     56.7
 4 1315934 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  23.5     70.3
 5 1315928 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  21.0     45.4
 6 1315926 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  25.9     66.2
 7 1315884 17 years old          Female 10th  White         White  51.4    132. 
 8 1315816 17 years old          Female 10th  All other ra… Mult…  22.2     59.0
 9 1315792 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  21.7     61.2
10 1315776 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  20.6     50.8
# ℹ 2,322 more rows



Note that we could also use the comma in this case:


yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female",    # comma is equivalent to & within filter()
         grade == "10th")

# A tibble: 2,332 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317010 18 years old or older Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  28.3     61.2
 2 1315996 17 years old          Female 10th  White         White  16.3     44.4
 3 1315971 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  19.6     56.7
 4 1315934 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  23.5     70.3
 5 1315928 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  21.0     45.4
 6 1315926 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  25.9     66.2
 7 1315884 17 years old          Female 10th  White         White  51.4    132. 
 8 1315816 17 years old          Female 10th  All other ra… Mult…  22.2     59.0
 9 1315792 17 years old          Female 10th  Hispanic/Lat… Hisp…  21.7     61.2
10 1315776 17 years old          Female 10th  Black or Afr… Blac…  20.6     50.8
# ℹ 2,322 more rows

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.5 Filtering with | (OR) criteria
If we wanted patients who were
Female or in 10th grade
we would use a | to chain these criteria together.

yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female" |
           grade == "10th")

# A tibble: 12,167 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…  17.7     49.9
 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other ra… Asian  19.8     44.4
10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     61.2
# ℹ 12,157 more rows



23.5.1 Think about it:
Which of the AND vs. OR code blocks will return a larger number of participants?

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.6 Your turn: filter()

Try itSolution


Using yrbss_data:

Keep only participants who are Female and in 12th grade.
Then keep only participants who are in 9th grade or have bmi greater than 30.




# AND: a comma (or &) inside filter()
yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female", grade == "12th")

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
23.7 More filter examples

See this slide from a previous BERD workshop.
Also read the filter section in the “Data Transformation” chapter from R for Data Science (2e).


23.7.1 More about comparison and logical operators
Click here for a useful reference for all the different operators (both logical and comparison) that you can use.


23.7.2 New since Feb 2026: filter_out()

filter_out() requires {dplyr} 1.2.0 or later. If you get could not find function "filter_out", update with update.packages() (or install.packages("dplyr")) and re-run library(tidyverse).


yrbss_data |>
  filter_out(sex == "Female") |> 
  tabyl(sex) # check 

  sex     n    percent valid_percent
 Male 10177 0.97780553             1
 <NA>   231 0.02219447            NA

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 23.8 Filtering out missing values using drop_na() (1/2)
text:
23.8 Filtering out missing values using drop_na() (1/2)

We may want to remove rows that have missing data, which are coded as NA.
See the drop_na() reference for examples (?drop_na).
First look at rates of missingness using the naniar package:


naniar::miss_var_summary(yrbss_data)

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  variable n_miss pct_miss
  <chr>     <int>    <num>
1 stweight   6557    32.8 
2 bmi        6458    32.3 
3 race7      1187     5.94
4 race4       710     3.55
5 grade       406     2.03
6 sex         231     1.16
7 age         205     1.03
8 record        0     0

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 23.9 Filtering out missing values using drop_na() (2/2)
text:
23.9 Filtering out missing values using drop_na() (2/2)
Pay attention to the number of rows for each of these results

Remove missingness in one column:


yrbss_data |> drop_na(race7)

 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…  17.7     49.9

10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     61.2

Remove all missingness:


yrbss_data |> drop_na()

# A tibble: 12,897 × 8
    record age                   sex    grade race4         race7   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…  24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…  17.7     49.9

10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White  21.7     61.2
# ℹ 12,887 more rows

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 23.10 A warning about filter(): check for NA
text:
23.10 A warning about filter(): check for NA

Suppose we want to remove the 10th grade category.
However this column has missingness in it as we can see with tabyl().
When we try to filter using !="10th" we lose our NAs!

That isn’t really expected.

You need to account for this with an or | statement:


yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(grade)

 grade    n percent valid_percent
  10th 4907 0.24535     0.2504338
  11th 4891 0.24455     0.2496172
  12th 4577 0.22885     0.2335919
   9th 5219 0.26095     0.2663570
  <NA>  406 0.02030            NA


Lost the NA’s in the filtering below!

yrbss_data |> 
  filter(grade != "10th") |>
  tabyl(grade)

 grade    n   percent
  11th 4891 0.3330156
  12th 4577 0.3116361
   9th 5219 0.3553483


Updated code to keep the NA’s when filtering:

yrbss_data |>
  # add an | (OR) statement 
  # to keep in the NA values
  filter((grade != "10th") |
           is.na(grade)) |>
  tabyl(grade)

 grade    n    percent valid_percent
  11th 4891 0.32405751     0.3330156
  12th 4577 0.30325316     0.3116361
   9th 5219 0.34578944     0.3553483
  <NA>  406 0.02689989            NA

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
24.1 Selecting columns using select()

The select() function allows us to select variables or columns from our dataset:


24.1.1 basic example

yrbss_data |>
  select(record, age, race4)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 3
    record age                   race4                    
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>                    
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Black or African American
 2 1317067 18 years old or older White                    
 3 1317066 18 years old or older White                    
 4 1317065 18 years old or older White                    
 5 1317064 18 years old or older White                    
 6 1317063 18 years old or older White                    
 7 1317062 18 years old or older White                    
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Black or African American
 9 1317057 18 years old or older All other races          
10 1317056 18 years old or older White                    
# ℹ 19,990 more rows




24.1.2 remove columns

If we want to select everything except specified variable(s),

we can use a - in front of the variable(s)



yrbss_data |>
  select(-race4, -race7)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 6
    record age                   sex    grade   bmi stweight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th   24.4     69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th   18.8     49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th   32.1     90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th   21.2     56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th   23.5     68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th   21.7     52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th   24.8     61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th   17.7     49.9
 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th   19.8     44.4
10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th   21.7     61.2
# ℹ 19,990 more rows




24.1.3 consecutive columns

Select consecutive columns with var_first:var_last
Make sure you know the order of your columns so that this is correct


yrbss_data |> select(age:grade)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 3
   age                   sex    grade
   <chr>                 <chr>  <chr>
 1 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 2 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 3 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 4 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 5 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 6 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 7 18 years old or older Female 12th 
 8 18 years old or older Female 11th 
 9 18 years old or older Female 12th 
10 18 years old or older Female 12th 
# ℹ 19,990 more rows




24.1.4 rearrange columns

select() and everything() are a useful combination for some quick rearranging of columns:


yrbss_data |> select(record, bmi, everything())

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
    record   bmi age                   sex    grade race4         race7 stweight
     <dbl> <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>         <chr>    <dbl>
 1 1317069  24.4 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afr… Blac…     69.0
 2 1317067  18.8 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     49.9
 3 1317066  32.1 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     90.7
 4 1317065  21.2 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     56.2
 5 1317064  23.5 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     68.0
 6 1317063  21.7 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     52.2
 7 1317062  24.8 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     61.2
 8 1317058  17.7 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afr… Blac…     49.9
 9 1317057  19.8 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other ra… Asian     44.4
10 1317056  21.7 18 years old or older Female 12th  White         White     61.2
# ℹ 19,990 more rows



There’s also a function relocate() to move around your column order.
The three options below all create the same results:


yrbss_data |> relocate(record, bmi, everything()) |> names()

[1] "record"   "bmi"      "age"      "sex"      "grade"    "race4"    "race7"   
[8] "stweight"

yrbss_data |> relocate(bmi, .after = record) |> names()

[1] "record"   "bmi"      "age"      "sex"      "grade"    "race4"    "race7"   
[8] "stweight"

yrbss_data |> relocate(bmi, .before = age) |> names()

[1] "record"   "bmi"      "age"      "sex"      "grade"    "race4"    "race7"   
[8] "stweight"

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
24.2 {tidyselect}

There are ways to select columns by “searching” for them.
These are called {tidyselect} helpers.
See more examples from the {tidyselect} webpage.


24.2.1 contains

For instance, you can see how this might be useful, where we select all columns where the column name includes the word “race”:


yrbss_data |> 
  select(contains("race"))

# A tibble: 20,000 × 2
   race4                     race7                    
   <chr>                     <chr>                    
 1 Black or African American Black or African American
 2 White                     White                    
 3 White                     White                    
 4 White                     White                    
 5 White                     White                    
 6 White                     White                    
 7 White                     White                    
 8 Black or African American Black or African American
 9 All other races           Asian                    
10 White                     White                    
# ℹ 19,990 more rows




24.2.2 starts_with, ends_with

Select all columns starting with “race”:


yrbss_data |> 
  select(starts_with("race")) |> 
  names()

[1] "race4" "race7"



Select all columns ending with “e”:


yrbss_data |> 
  select(ends_with("e")) |> 
  names()

[1] "age"   "grade"




24.2.3 Select columns by data type

We can also select variables based on data types
For instance, select(where(is.numeric)) selects all numeric columns

(where() is a helper function that returns columns where the inside function is TRUE,
in this case is.numeric returns the TRUE/FALSE)



yrbss_data |> 
  select(where(is.numeric)) |> 
  names()

[1] "record"   "bmi"      "stweight"

yrbss_data |> 
  select(where(is.character)) |> 
  names()

[1] "age"   "sex"   "grade" "race4" "race7"

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 24.3 The difference between filter() and select()
text:
24.3 The difference between filter() and select()

One thing to keep in mind is that:

filter() works on rows (think FILTER in Excel!), and
select() works on columns (select your relevant variables)


Keep that in mind!

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
24.4 Renaming with rename()

You can rename columns with select() but it’s a bit easier to do this with the rename() function.
You just need to remember new_name = old_name ordering.

One equal sign will suffice!



yrbss_data |> rename(id = record,
                  race_cat = race4,
                  weight = stweight)

# A tibble: 20,000 × 8
        id age                   sex    grade race_cat        race7   bmi weight
     <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>  <chr> <chr>           <chr> <dbl>  <dbl>
 1 1317069 18 years old or older Female 12th  Black or Afric… Blac…  24.4   69.0
 2 1317067 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  18.8   49.9
 3 1317066 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  32.1   90.7
 4 1317065 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  21.2   56.2
 5 1317064 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  23.5   68.0
 6 1317063 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  21.7   52.2
 7 1317062 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  24.8   61.2
 8 1317058 18 years old or older Female 11th  Black or Afric… Blac…  17.7   49.9
 9 1317057 18 years old or older Female 12th  All other races Asian  19.8   44.4
10 1317056 18 years old or older Female 12th  White           White  21.7   61.2
# ℹ 19,990 more rows


Note that we did not save the changes above, and thus we haven’t actually renamed the variables in the dataset yrbss_data!

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 24.5 Saving our results as a file
text:
24.5 Saving our results as a file

Let’s save some processed data in the output/ directory.
We’ll save it as an xlsx file so that we can open it in Excel.


# chain together several data transformation steps with |>

# use <- to assign the new data to a different name to avoid overwriting the original dataset

processed_data <- yrbss_data |>   
  select(-race7) |>
  rename(id = record,
         race_cat = race4,
         weight = stweight) |>
  filter(sex == "Female")

glimpse(processed_data)

Rows: 9,592
Columns: 7
$ id       <dbl> 1317069, 1317067, 1317066, 1317065, 1317064, 1317063, 1317062…
$ age      <chr> "18 years old or older", "18 years old or older", "18 years o…
$ sex      <chr> "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "…
$ grade    <chr> "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "11th…
$ race_cat <chr> "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whit…
$ bmi      <dbl> 24.4296, 18.7813, 32.1429, 21.1713, 23.5433, 21.7107, 24.8448…
$ weight   <dbl> 68.95, 49.90, 90.72, 56.25, 68.04, 52.16, 61.24, 49.90, 44.45…


Save the processed_data:

write_xlsx(x = processed_data,
                path = "output/processed_data.xlsx")

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 26.1 mutate() - A confusing name, a powerful {dplyr} verb
text:
26.1 mutate() - A confusing name, a powerful {dplyr} verb

You can use mutate() to

transform existing columns (variables in your dataset) and/or
add new columns to the dataset.


Think of this like adding a formula in Excel to calculate the value of a new column based on previous columns. You can do lots of things such as:

subtract one column from another
convert the units of one column to new units (such as days to years)
change the capitalization of categories in a variable
recode a continuous variable to be a categorical one

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
section: 26.2 Calculate a new variable based on other variables with mutate
text:
26.2 Calculate a new variable based on other variables with mutate

26.2.1 Example 1

Convert the weights from kg to lbs, since stweight is recorded in kilograms


yrbss_data |> 
    mutate(weight_lb = stweight*2.205) |>
    glimpse()

Rows: 20,000
Columns: 9
$ record    <dbl> 1317069, 1317067, 1317066, 1317065, 1317064, 1317063, 131706…
$ age       <chr> "18 years old or older", "18 years old or older", "18 years …
$ sex       <chr> "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", …
$ grade     <chr> "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "11t…
$ race4     <chr> "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi…
$ race7     <chr> "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi…
$ bmi       <dbl> 24.4296, 18.7813, 32.1429, 21.1713, 23.5433, 21.7107, 24.844…
$ stweight  <dbl> 68.95, 49.90, 90.72, 56.25, 68.04, 52.16, 61.24, 49.90, 44.4…
$ weight_lb <dbl> 152.03475, 110.02950, 200.03760, 124.03125, 150.02820, 115.0…



Notice we use = inside mutate, not == or <-.
We are not making a logical operation, nor are we assigning something to an object name.

Since we haven’t assigned this new “mutated” dataset to an object name, our work hasn’t been saved.


26.2.2 Example 2

Convert the weights from kg to lbs, since stweight is recorded in kilograms (same as in Ex 1)
Convert the weights to g, using the kg weights
Create a new dataset (yrbss_new) with the new variables


yrbss_new <- yrbss_data |> 
    mutate(weight_lb = stweight*2.205,
           weight_g = stweight*1000)

glimpse(yrbss_new)

Rows: 20,000
Columns: 10
$ record    <dbl> 1317069, 1317067, 1317066, 1317065, 1317064, 1317063, 131706…
$ age       <chr> "18 years old or older", "18 years old or older", "18 years …
$ sex       <chr> "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", "Female", …
$ grade     <chr> "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "12th", "11t…
$ race4     <chr> "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi…
$ race7     <chr> "Black or African American", "White", "White", "White", "Whi…
$ bmi       <dbl> 24.4296, 18.7813, 32.1429, 21.1713, 23.5433, 21.7107, 24.844…
$ stweight  <dbl> 68.95, 49.90, 90.72, 56.25, 68.04, 52.16, 61.24, 49.90, 44.4…
$ weight_lb <dbl> 152.03475, 110.02950, 200.03760, 124.03125, 150.02820, 115.0…
$ weight_g  <dbl> 68950, 49900, 90720, 56250, 68040, 52160, 61240, 49900, 4445…



We saved the new dataset as yrbss_new

to avoid overwriting our other data set.
Check out the environment tab to see the new dataset listed.

Notice how we can add multiple new columns within one mutate() function.

We can also mutate using multiple columns.
Here we have created two new weight variables in different scales
In the next Practice time, you will make a height variable from bmi and stweight




26.2.3 Examine new columns
Figure comparing the newly created weight_lb column to the original stweight column:

ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = stweight, 
      y = weight_lb) +
  geom_point()

Warning: Removed 6557 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
26.3 Categorical data (factors)

26.3.1 Intro

One data type that we haven’t yet looked at are factor variables

factors are how R represents levels of categorical data.

For the most part, you can use character and factors interchangeably for categorical data.
However, there are two main differences:

factors define the permissible values in a vector with an argument called levels.
The levels define the order in which the permissible values are displayed, such as in a table or figure.
The default order of levels (values) is in alphanumeric order.




26.3.2 Make grade factor

Below we use the factor() function within a mutate() to transform grade from a character variable into a factor variable.


class(yrbss_new$grade) # what's the data type of grade?

[1] "character"

yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade)

 grade    n percent valid_percent
  10th 4907 0.24535     0.2504338
  11th 4891 0.24455     0.2496172
  12th 4577 0.22885     0.2335919
   9th 5219 0.26095     0.2663570
  <NA>  406 0.02030            NA

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  #create a factor variable
  mutate(grade_fac1 = factor(grade)) 

# Checks:
class(yrbss_new$grade_fac1) # what's the data type of grade_fac1?

[1] "factor"

levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac1) # what are its levels?

[1] "10th" "11th" "12th" "9th" 

# values are the same in the two grade variables:
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade, grade_fac1) |> adorn_title()

       grade_fac1                   
 grade       10th 11th 12th  9th NA_
  10th       4907    0    0    0   0
  11th          0 4891    0    0   0
  12th          0    0 4577    0   0
   9th          0    0    0 5219   0
  <NA>          0    0    0    0 406




26.3.3 Change order of levels

Use the levels argument within the factor() function

Example: Create grade_fac2 variable as a factor version of grade with grades in “correct” order:

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  #create a factor variable
  mutate(grade_fac2 = factor(
    grade, 
    levels = c("9th","10th","11th","12th")
    ))

# Checks:
class(yrbss_new$grade_fac2) # what's the data type of grade_fac1?

[1] "factor"

levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac2) # what are its levels?

[1] "9th"  "10th" "11th" "12th"

yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac2)

 grade_fac2    n percent valid_percent
        9th 5219 0.26095     0.2663570
       10th 4907 0.24535     0.2504338
       11th 4891 0.24455     0.2496172
       12th 4577 0.22885     0.2335919
       <NA>  406 0.02030            NA

# values are the same in the two grade factor variables:
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac1, grade_fac2) |> adorn_title()

            grade_fac2                   
 grade_fac1        9th 10th 11th 12th NA_
       10th          0 4907    0    0   0
       11th          0    0 4891    0   0
       12th          0    0    0 4577   0
        9th       5219    0    0    0   0
       <NA>          0    0    0    0 406




26.3.4 Be careful!

Recall that the levels of a factor are the permissible values in a factor.

Spelling matters!

If we try to use a factor level that doesn’t exist (“10” vs “10th”),

we won’t have any data in that category, and
the non-specified levels (“10”) get set to NA (missing).



yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  mutate(grade_fac3 = factor(
    grade, 
    levels = c("9th","10","11th","12th") # typo!!
    )) 

# check
levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac3) # what are its levels?

[1] "9th"  "10"   "11th" "12th"

yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac3)

 grade_fac3    n percent valid_percent
        9th 5219 0.26095     0.3553483
         10    0 0.00000     0.0000000
       11th 4891 0.24455     0.3330156
       12th 4577 0.22885     0.3116361
       <NA> 5313 0.26565            NA

# compare
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac2, grade_fac3) |> adorn_title()

            grade_fac3                  
 grade_fac2        9th 10 11th 12th  NA_
        9th       5219  0    0    0    0
       10th          0  0    0    0 4907
       11th          0  0 4891    0    0
       12th          0  0    0 4577    0
       <NA>          0  0    0    0  406




26.3.5 Replace grade with factor version

Below we are doing something called reassignment:

Weâre taking the previous values of our variable (grade),
doing something to them (making them a factor), and
then reassigning the variable grade to its new set of values.

I don’t recommend replacing a variable with new values until you are 100% positive that your code is correct!!

I can’t compare the new grade values to the original ones anymore to check my work



yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  mutate(grade = factor(
    grade, 
    levels = c("9th","10th","11th","12th")
    )) 

# compare to grade_fac2 (which we determined to be correct)
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade, grade_fac2) |> adorn_title()

       grade_fac2                   
 grade        9th 10th 11th 12th NA_
   9th       5219    0    0    0   0
  10th          0 4907    0    0   0
  11th          0    0 4891    0   0
  12th          0    0    0 4577   0
  <NA>          0    0    0    0 406




26.3.6 Remove extra variables

This step isn’t necessary, but we don’t need the columns grade_fac1, grade_fac1-grade_fac3 anymore.


names(yrbss_new)

 [1] "record"     "age"        "sex"        "grade"      "race4"     
 [6] "race7"      "bmi"        "stweight"   "weight_lb"  "weight_g"  
[11] "grade_fac1" "grade_fac2" "grade_fac3"

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> select(-grade_fac1:-grade_fac3)
names(yrbss_new)

 [1] "record"    "age"       "sex"       "grade"     "race4"     "race7"    
 [7] "bmi"       "stweight"  "weight_lb" "weight_g"

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
28.1 case_when()

28.1.1 Intro

How can we dichotomize a continuous variable, such as BMI?
How can we combine categories of a variable, such as race?
How can we convert categories to numbers, such as sex?

These are all examples where case_when() can be used.


28.1.2 Code

The code follows a basic pattern for each category inside case_when():

condition ~ category name

The left side of the ~ is where we specify how to define the category.
The right side of the ~ is where we specify the category name.
Separate each “case” with a comma.



28.1.3 Example 1

yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex)

    sex     n percent valid_percent
 Female  9592 0.47960     0.4852041
   Male 10177 0.50885     0.5147959
   <NA>   231 0.01155            NA



Convert sex to a new numeric variable called female whose values are

1 when sex is Female, and
0 when sex is Male


left side defines the condition ~ right side names the category

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    female = case_when(
      # when sex is Female, make female equal to 1 
      sex == "Female" ~ 1, # do not forget this comma!
      
      # when sex is Male, make female equal to 0
      sex == "Male" ~ 0
      )
    ) 

# checks
yrbss_new |> tabyl(female) # look at the categories

 female     n percent valid_percent
      0 10177 0.50885     0.5147959
      1  9592 0.47960     0.4852041
     NA   231 0.01155            NA

class(yrbss_new$female)

[1] "numeric"

# compare female and sex variables
yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex, female) |> adorn_title()

        female         
    sex      0    1 NA_
 Female      0 9592   0
   Male  10177    0   0
   <NA>      0    0 231



How were the missing values (NA) handled?



28.1.4 Example 2

Dichotomize the numeric variable bmi into two categories with 30 as the cutoff.

left side defines the condition ~ right side names the category

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    bmi30 = case_when(
      bmi < 30 ~ "<30", # don't forget a comma between cases
      bmi >= 30 ~ "30+"
      ) 
  ) 

# check
yrbss_new |> tabyl(bmi30)

 bmi30     n percent valid_percent
   30+  1351 0.06755     0.0997637
   <30 12191 0.60955     0.9002363
  <NA>  6458 0.32290            NA

class(yrbss_new$bmi30)

[1] "character"

# check values were categorized correctly: (look at mins & maxes)
yrbss_new |> 
  group_by(bmi30) |> 
  get_summary_stats(bmi) |> 
  gt()

Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
ℹ In argument: `sd = stats::sd(.value., na.rm = TRUE)`.
Caused by warning:
! There were 2 warnings in `dplyr::summarise()`.
The first warning was:
ℹ In argument: `min = min(.value., na.rm = TRUE)`.
Caused by warning in `min()`:

28.1.5 Example 3

Recode bmi to have 4 levels:

<20 (not including exactly 20)
20-30 (not including exactly 30)
30+
unknown if missing

Code comments

Notice that for the middle category we use an & statement similar to how we used logic in filter
New: used .default option to specify what to assign in all other unspecified cases.



yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    bmi3cat = case_when(
      bmi < 20 ~ "<20",
      (bmi >=20) & (bmi < 30) ~ "20-30",
      bmi >= 30 ~ "30+",
      .default = "unknown"  # apply this in all other cases
      ),
    bmi3cat = factor(bmi3cat) # make it factor after creating categories
    )
  
# check

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
30.1 replace_na()

Sometimes we want to fill in missing values with a certain value.
We can use the replace_na() function inside of mutate() to specify this.


30.1.1 Example 1

For example, suppose if the bmi variable is missing,

we want to replace it with the average bmi across the entire data set.



yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    bmi_filled = replace_na(
      bmi,
      mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE))
    )           

# check
yrbss_new |> 
  select(contains("bmi")) |> 
  tail()

# A tibble: 6 × 4
    bmi bmi30 bmi3cat bmi_filled
  <dbl> <chr> <fct>        <dbl>
1    NA <NA>  unknown       23.5
2    NA <NA>  unknown       23.5
3    NA <NA>  unknown       23.5
4    NA <NA>  unknown       23.5
5    NA <NA>  unknown       23.5
6    NA <NA>  unknown       23.5

# how does bmi_filled compare to bmi?
yrbss_new |> 
  select(bmi, bmi_filled) |> 
  get_summary_stats() |> 
  select(-iqr, -se, -ci) |> 
  gt()

30.1.2 Example 2

We can also fill in missing values for character variables
Here we replace missingness in the sex column with unknown:


class(yrbss_new$sex)

[1] "character"

yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex)

    sex     n percent valid_percent
 Female  9592 0.47960     0.4852041
   Male 10177 0.50885     0.5147959
   <NA>   231 0.01155            NA

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
    mutate(
      sexfilled = replace_na(sex, "unknown"))

# check
yrbss_new |> tabyl(sexfilled, sex) |> adorn_title()

              sex          
 sexfilled Female  Male NA_
    Female   9592     0   0
      Male      0 10177   0
   unknown      0     0 231




30.1.3 Example 3

What happens when we use replace_na() to fill in missing values for factor variables?


class(yrbss_new$grade)

[1] "factor"



yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
    mutate(
      gradefilled = replace_na(grade, "unknown"))

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
30.2 Your turn: replace_na()

Try itSolution


Using yrbss_new:

Create a new variable stweight_filled that replaces missing stweight values with the median stweight.
Compare stweight and stweight_filled to check your work.




yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(stweight_filled = replace_na(stweight, median(stweight, na.rm = TRUE)))

# check: the filled version has no missing values
yrbss_new |>
  select(stweight, stweight_filled) |>
  get_summary_stats(type = "common")

# A tibble: 2 × 10
  variable            n   min   max median   iqr  mean    sd    se    ci
  <fct>           <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
31.1 Boxplots with geom_boxplot()

Boxplots can be used to compare the distribution of a quantitative variable among categories.

We need a categorical variable (here, our x axis)
and a continuous numeric variable, shown here on our y axis.



# creating a boxplot

ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = grade, 
      y = bmi) +
  geom_boxplot()

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).








# Note that the grades are in the correct order now!


The main differences from the scatterplots we created earlier are the geom type and the variables plotted.
We can change the color similarly to scatterplots.

However, we map to fill and not color if we want to fill in the box with color:



# adding color
ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = grade, 
      y = bmi,
      fill = grade) +
  geom_boxplot()

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
31.2 Faceting our boxplot with facet_wrap()

One of the most powerful ways to change a visualization is by faceting.

We can make multiple plots stratifying by a categorical variable.

To do this, we have to add the facet_wrap() function to our plot.

We need to specify the variable to facet_wrap - race4 by using the vars() function to specify it as a variable.



# facet_wrap:
ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = bmi, 
      y = grade,
      fill = grade) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(vars(factor(race4)))  #NEW!

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).










Don’t try to facet on a continuous numeric variable - it won’t work.
Don’t forget to look at the help documentation (e.g., ?facet_wrap) to learn more about additional ways to customize your plots!

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
31.3 Faceting our boxplot with facet_grid()

Another way to facet our plots is with facet_grid(),

which lets us select the rows and columns based on categorical variables:



ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = bmi, 
      y = sex,
      fill = sex) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(grade),  #NEW!
             cols = vars(race4))

Warning: Removed 6458 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_boxplot()`).

title: Code for Data Wrangling & Summarizing in R
text:
31.4 Mini-practice
Facet the boxplot below by age and grade, don’t forget the vars():

# change eval: TRUE above

ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  
  aes(x = bmi, 
      y = sex,
      fill = sex) +
  
  geom_boxplot() + 
  
  facet_grid(______)

section: 34.1 You WILL get frustrated while learning R!
text:
34.1 You WILL get frustrated while learning R!
From Garrett Grolemund’s Prologue of his book Hands-On Programming with R1:

As you learn to program, you are going to get frustrated. You are learning a new language, and it will take time to become fluent. But frustration is not just natural, itâs actually a positive sign that you should watch for. Frustration is your brainâs way of being lazy; itâs trying to get you to quit and go do something easy or fun. If you want to get physically fitter, you need to push your body even though it complains. If you want to get better at programming, youâll need to push your brain. Recognize when you get frustrated and see it as a good thing: youâre now stretching yourself. Push yourself a little further every day, and youâll soon be a confident programmer.
